g.data-narration: Welcome to Shell Programming - the fundamental skill for Unix/Linux system automation and scripting.
A shell is both a command interpreter and a programming language that provides interface to Unix/Linux kernel services.
g.text: • Enables task automation via shell scripts

g.data-narration: Four major shell types: Bourne Shell (original), Bash (most popular), C Shell (C-like syntax), and Korn Shell (commercial).
Shell variables store data as strings. Assign using equals without spaces, access with dollar prefix. Variables can be system or user defined.
Special variables have predefined meanings: zero is script name, one to n are arguments, double dollar is PID, question mark is exit status zero equals success.
If-else provides conditional execution. Note spaces inside brackets, use fi to close. Supports elif for multiple conditions.
Case statement provides multi-way branching using pattern matching. Use esac to close, wildcards asterisk allowed.
Three loop types: for iterates over lists, while loops while condition true, until loops until condition becomes true.
Command substitution captures command output as values. Use dollar parentheses command or backticks. Prefer dollar parentheses for readability and nesting.
I/O redirection controls input output: greater than writes to file, double greater than appends, less than reads from file, two greater than redirects errors, pipe symbol pipes commands.
Functions enable modular code. Use local keyword for scoped variables. Parameters passed as one two, return with exit code.
Arithmetic uses double parentheses expression. Arrays declared with parentheses, accessed via index. Hash array at gives array length.
This script prompts for username, validates input, checks et cetera passwd, and displays user details or error message.
This grade calculator uses case statement to map marks ranges to grades S A B C D F with pattern matching.
Key takeaways: Always use spaces in conditionals, quote variables, remember exit status zero equals success, close blocks with fi esac.
g.text: fi closes if, esac closes case

g.data-narration: Master shell programming to automate tasks, process files, and administer systems effectively. Practice writing scripts regularly.
g.text: Automation is the key to efficient system administration
